intent:
Zoom ALL the way out on a big library — the state the user actually
described, and the one `zoomed_out_thumb_storm.toml` failed to reach (it
zoomed 4 steps and recorded `evictions=0`, i.e. it never even filled the
atlas).

The hypothesis this tests is a **runaway regeneration loop**, not a
throttle problem:

  1. zoom out until visible tiles exceed the atlas slot count (12×12 = 144)
  2. every visible tile is an in-zone static, so `alloc_slot` classifies
     them all class 0 and an incoming static needs class ≥ 1 — it can
     evict NOTHING and returns None
  3. `drain_media` therefore throws the finished artifact away and resets
     the clip to `Thumb::None` ("stay retryable")
  4. the clip is still visible, so `request_visible_thumbs` re-queues it on
     the very next frame
  5. goto 3 — forever, at whatever rate the pool can sustain

On a local SSD the redo is a cache `hit` (jpeg decode, no source I/O) and
the loop is merely wasteful. On SMB every redo needs `entry_dir()` →
`metadata()` on the SOURCE volume for the size_mtime cache key, so it is a
network round-trip per retry; and on a COLD library the artifact does not
exist yet, so every retry is a full `made` job — seek + decode over SMB,
which never converges.

Read:
  - **`atlas_full_drops`** (new) — the canary. Non-zero means the loop is
    running; climbing without bound means it will never stop on its own.
  - `thumb_requests` climbing while `thumbs_cached` plateaus — the same
    loop from the other end. Compare the ratio against `zoomed_in_control`,
    where they should track each other.
  - `jobs` with `hit` vs `made` split — on a cold library these retries are
    `made`, which is what saturates the link.
  - `queue_wait: thumb` — with the loop running, a genuinely new visible
    thumb queues behind an endless stream of retries.
  - `decode reads` / `render_stall_*` / `tick_ms` — whether playback and
    the UI survive it.

The zoom is applied as a stream of many presses with no settle, then held,
so the run measures the steady state of the loop rather than its onset.
